articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Escambia County's $47 Million Stormwater Problem Has No Fix in Sight
articles.dek: Three years after voters approved a dedicated fee, the county has spent more on consultants than construction. Records show why.
brief: Escambia County has collected $47.2 million in stormwater fees since 2023 but completed only 3 of 28 planned drainage projects
Public records show $8.1 million spent on engineering consultants and project management while residents report worsening flooding
County officials blame permitting delays and contractor shortages, but internal emails reveal deeper dysfunction in project prioritization
articles.body:
The water rises fast on Doris Drive. Three inches of rain fell the morning of March 8, and by noon Linda Harrelson was watching it creep across her garage floor for the fourth time in two years.

"They promised us," Harrelson said, standing in her driveway in the Brownsville neighborhood. "We voted for that fee because they said it would fix this."

She's talking about the stormwater utility fee Escambia County voters approved in November 2022. The referendum passed with 61% support after county officials presented a detailed plan: $15 million annually dedicated exclusively to drainage improvements. No more raiding the general fund. No more deferred maintenance. A 10-year capital plan targeting the county's worst flood zones.

Three and a half years later, records obtained by The Flightline through public records requests tell a different story.

The Numbers

Since collection began in January 2023, the stormwater utility has generated $47.2 million in revenue. Of that amount:

  • $8.1 million has gone to engineering consultants, project managers and feasibility studies
  • $4.3 million covers administrative costs including billing, collections and a new software system
  • $6.8 million sits in reserve accounts
  • $11.4 million has been spent on actual construction

The remaining $16.6 million is allocated to projects in various stages of design or permitting—none with scheduled construction start dates.

Of the 28 drainage projects identified in the original 2022 capital plan, three have been completed. All three were already partially designed before the fee took effect.

What Went Wrong

County Administrator Wes Moreno points to factors beyond the county's control.

"We're competing for contractors with massive federal infrastructure projects across the Gulf Coast," Moreno said in an interview. "FDOT work, hurricane recovery, port expansion—everyone's bidding on the same limited pool."

He also cited permitting delays at the Northwest Florida Water Management District, which must approve projects affecting wetlands or waterways. The district's average review time has stretched from 90 days to nearly seven months.

But internal emails obtained through public records requests suggest the problems started closer to home.

In March 2024, Public Works Director Tammy Richardson sent an email to Moreno flagging "significant concerns" about project sequencing. The county had hired three separate engineering firms to design projects in the same drainage basin without coordinating their work.

"We now have conflicting designs that cannot all be built," Richardson wrote. "We need to stop and reassess before we waste more money."

The reassessment took eight months. During that time, the county paid all three firms to continue preliminary work on projects that may never be constructed.

Richardson declined to comment for this article, referring questions to the county's communications office.

The Consultant Question

The $8.1 million in consulting fees has drawn particular scrutiny from residents who attend county commission meetings.

Approximately $3.2 million went to Jacobs Engineering for a countywide stormwater master plan completed in September 2024. The document runs 847 pages and identifies 156 potential projects ranked by cost-effectiveness and flood reduction impact.

The county then hired a different firm, Volkert Inc., for $1.8 million to create a "prioritization matrix" that re-ranked the same projects using different criteria.

"So we paid $5 million for two lists that don't agree with each other," said Marcus Webb, a Brent resident who has attended every commission meeting since 2024. "And meanwhile the ditch behind my house hasn't been cleaned in three years."

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose district includes some of the county's worst flooding areas, defended the studies.

"You can't just start digging ditches without engineering," Bergosh said. "These systems are interconnected. Fix one thing wrong and you flood someone else."

He acknowledged frustration with the pace of progress but said the county is "turning a corner."

"We're past the planning phase. You'll see shovels in the ground this year."

The Completed Projects

The three finished projects tell their own story.

A $2.1 million culvert replacement on Nine Mile Road was substantially designed before the 2022 referendum. Construction began in April 2023 and finished that October. Residents report it has reduced flooding on adjacent streets.

A $1.4 million drainage improvement in the Ensley area followed a similar trajectory—pre-designed and shovel-ready when funding became available.

The third project, a $890,000 retention pond expansion near Olive Road, took 18 months to complete after the contractor encountered unexpected utility conflicts that required redesign.

No other projects have broken ground.

What's Next

The county's current capital plan shows seven projects scheduled to begin construction in 2026. Combined, they represent $14.2 million in estimated costs.

But the plan comes with caveats. All seven projects require permits the county has not yet received. Two depend on acquiring private property through negotiated purchase or eminent domain. One requires coordination with the Florida Department of Transportation on a state road.

Commissioner Steven Barry, who voted against the original stormwater fee referendum language, has called for an independent audit.

"The voters were told exactly what their money would buy and when," Barry said at a March commission meeting. "We owe them an honest accounting of why we haven't delivered."

The commission has not scheduled a vote on the audit proposal.

On Doris Drive, Harrelson has stopped waiting for answers. She's gotten quotes from contractors to raise her garage floor six inches. The work will cost $8,400.

"That's about what I've paid in stormwater fees since this started," she said. "At least I know where my money's going."

articles.label: Feature
articles.headline: The Last Fish House on Bayou Chico
articles.dek: Joe Patti's has outlasted hurricanes, highway expansions, and a waterfront that barely resembles what his grandfather knew. Now comes the next test.
brief: Joe Patti's Seafood remains the sole working fish house on Bayou Chico after decades of waterfront transformation
Third-generation owner Frank Patti faces rising insurance costs and pressure from mixed-use development proposals
The family has rejected multiple buyout offers as neighboring properties convert to condos and marinas
articles.body:
The ice truck backs up to the loading dock at 4:47 a.m., same as it has six days a week for longer than anyone currently working here can remember. Frank Patti stands in the floodlit doorway of the processing room, watching his crew unload the night's haul from three boats. Red snapper. Triggerfish. A decent run of grouper. The bay is still black beyond the dock lights, and the air smells like diesel and salt and fish guts.

His grandfather started this operation in 1931. Back then, a dozen fish houses lined Bayou Chico. Oystermen worked the bars. Shrimp boats crowded the channel. The waterfront was a working waterfront, not a phrase anyone needed to use because there wasn't any other kind.

Now Joe Patti's stands alone.

"Look around," Frank Patti says, gesturing with a coffee cup toward the bayou. "Tell me what you see."

What you see: a 14-story condominium tower to the east, completed in 2024. A marina with 200 slips, most occupied by boats that never leave the dock. A shuttered restaurant that used to be a net house. A city park built on a former boatyard. The skeleton of another condo project rising to the west, steel beams catching the first gray light.

The fish house sits between them like something left behind.

Ninety-five years of storms

The original structure went up the year after the 1926 hurricane. Joe Patti, an Italian immigrant who'd been selling fish from a cart, figured the bayou offered protection the open waterfront didn't. He was half right. The location shielded the business from the worst storm surge, but hurricanes still came.

Ivan in 2004 flooded the building to the ceiling. Sally in 2020 dropped four feet of water and took the roof off the cold storage. Each time, the family rebuilt. The current structure—a sprawl of concrete block, corrugated metal, and additions that span five decades—shows the scars. Waterlines are visible on the older walls if you know where to look.

Frank Patti, 61, took over from his father in 1998. He's the last of the line working the business. His children have other careers. His cousins sold their shares years ago. The succession question hangs over everything.

"I don't think about it," he says, which is not the same as saying it doesn't matter.

The offers keep coming

The Patti family owns 4.2 acres along the bayou, including the fish house, retail market, parking lots, and a strip of waterfront that developers have been circling for two decades. Escambia County property records show the combined parcels assessed at $8.3 million, though everyone involved acknowledges that figure understates the development potential.

Frank Patti confirms the family has received "multiple" buyout offers in recent years. He won't name figures or parties. He will say they've rejected all of them.

"This isn't a real estate play for us," he says. "We sell fish."

The retail market moves 12,000 to 15,000 pounds on a typical Saturday, according to Patti. The wholesale operation supplies restaurants across the Panhandle. The business employs 43 people full-time, plus seasonal workers. It is, by any measure, a going concern.

But the economics have shifted. Commercial fishing permits in the Gulf are finite and expensive. Fuel costs have tripled since 2019. And then there's insurance.

Patti's property insurance premium increased 340 percent between 2022 and 2025, according to documents he provided to The Flightline. The current annual premium exceeds $890,000. For a business built on thin margins—fish is perishable, prices fluctuate, one bad storm can wipe out a season—the number lands like a body blow.

"That's not sustainable," he says. "Something has to give."

What the city sees

Pensacola's waterfront development strategy, outlined in the 2023 comprehensive plan update, emphasizes "activating" underutilized parcels along the bay and bayous. The language is careful—the plan acknowledges the value of "maritime heritage" and "working waterfront uses"—but the direction is clear. The city wants density. It wants tax revenue. It wants the kind of mixed-use projects that have transformed downtowns from Tampa to Charleston.

Bayou Chico fits the vision. The bayou connects to Pensacola Bay, which connects to the Gulf. The views are unobstructed. The land is flat and buildable. And much of it has already converted.

City Council member Jared Moore, whose District 6 includes the Bayou Chico waterfront, says he's not aware of any active proposal involving the Joe Patti's property. But he acknowledges the broader pressure.

"Market forces are what they are," Moore said in an interview. "We can't force anyone to keep operating a fish house. What we can do is make sure the zoning reflects the community's values."

The current zoning on the Patti parcels is Heavy Commercial-Waterfront, a designation that permits industrial maritime uses. Changing that would require a formal application, public hearings, and council approval. No such application has been filed.

The waterfront that was

Harold Ewing, 78, worked at Joe Patti's for 41 years before retiring in 2019. On a Tuesday morning, he's sitting in the retail market, eating boiled shrimp and watching tourists fill their coolers.

"This used to be the ugly part of town," he says. "Fish smell, diesel fumes, bait everywhere. Nobody wanted to live here."

He recites the names of operations that are gone. Sanders Seafood. Gulf Fish Company. Bayou Chico Oyster. The net makers. The ice plants. The fuel docks that served the shrimp fleet.

"All of it, gone," he says. "Just us left."

The transformation accelerated after Hurricane Ivan. Damaged properties sold cheap to investors who waited out the recovery, then built upward. The first condos came in 2008. The marina followed. Each project raised property values, which raised taxes, which made it harder for low-margin businesses to hold on.

Ewing doesn't romanticize the old waterfront. The work was brutal. The pay was thin. The smell, genuinely terrible.

"But it was real," he says. "People made a living. Fish came off boats and went to tables. You could see it happen."

What comes next

Frank Patti is standing at the edge of the dock, watching a trawler tie up. The captain, a third-generation fisherman named Luis Reyes, has been selling to Joe Patti's for 22 years. He's got a hold full of Spanish mackerel.

"How much longer you gonna do this?" Patti asks.

Reyes shrugs. "How much longer you gonna buy it?"

This is the question neither man can answer. The commercial fishing fleet in Pensacola has shrunk by more than half since 2000, according to Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission data. Permits aren't being renewed. Young people aren't entering the trade. The boats that remain are older, their owners aging out.

A fish house needs fish. If the boats stop coming, the rest is arithmetic.

Patti has considered alternatives. Aquaculture. Imported product to supplement the local catch. Expanding the retail side, which carries higher margins. None of it solves the insurance problem. None of it answers the succession question.

"My grandfather came here with nothing," he says. "Sold fish off a cart. Built this. Survived the Depression, survived the wars, survived every storm. I'm not the one who's going to sell it to some developer so they can build another condo."

He pauses.

"But I'm also not the one who gets to decide what happens after I'm gone."

The sun is fully up now, burning off the morning haze. The retail market opens in an hour. The ice machine hums. The crew hoses down the dock. Inside, staff are laying out the day's catch on ice—snapper, grouper, shrimp, oysters—the same as they have for 95 years.

The last fish house on Bayou Chico opens for business.

articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Blue Angels Homecoming Week Brings 300,000 Visitors and One Big Question for Beach Residents
articles.dek: Short-term rental enforcement goes dark during the July show. Pensacola Beach homeowners want to know who made that call.
brief: Santa Rosa Island Authority paused short-term rental enforcement during the 2025 Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show, allowing unlicensed properties to operate without inspection or citation.
Permanent beach residents say the decision flooded neighborhoods with unvetted renters while official channels stayed silent about the policy change.
SRIA officials have not responded to public records requests seeking documentation of who authorized the enforcement pause or why.
articles.body:
The knock never came.

For three years, Linda Carswell has watched the same unlicensed rental house on Ariola Drive fill up every Blue Angels weekend. Strangers hauling coolers across her lawn to the beach access. Cars parked on the sandy shoulder until they sink. Music until 2 a.m. She's filed complaints. She's called the Santa Rosa Island Authority. She's done everything the system asks.

During the 2025 Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show last July, the house next door operated at full capacity. No inspector knocked. No citation appeared in the SRIA's public database. The weekend came and went.

"I called Monday morning," Carswell said. "They told me they weren't doing enforcement that week. Nobody told the residents that. Nobody asked us."

The Quiet Pause

The Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show draws an estimated 300,000 visitors to Pensacola Beach over two days each July. Hotels sell out months ahead. Licensed vacation rentals book a year in advance. The economic impact, according to Visit Pensacola, exceeds $50 million annually for Escambia and Santa Rosa counties combined.

The Santa Rosa Island Authority, which governs land use and code enforcement on Pensacola Beach, operates a short-term rental registration program requiring property owners to obtain permits, pass safety inspections, and maintain liability insurance. Unlicensed rentals face fines starting at $500 per violation.

During Homecoming week 2025, that enforcement apparatus went silent.

The Flightline submitted a public records request in August 2025 seeking any documentation of a formal policy suspending enforcement during the air show period. As of publication, SRIA has not produced responsive records. A follow-up request in January 2026 remains pending.

SRIA Executive Director Paolo Ghio did not respond to three interview requests for this article. Board Chair Brigette Brooks declined to comment, referring questions to Ghio's office.

What Residents Saw

Tom and Marie Hennessey have owned their home on Ensenada Marbella for 22 years. They moved to Pensacola Beach permanently in 2019. They describe Homecoming weekend as the annual stress test for their block.

"You know which houses are licensed," Tom Hennessey said. "They have the little signs. They follow the rules. It's the others we worry about."

The Hennesseys counted four properties on their street operating as apparent rentals during the 2025 show. Two lacked visible SRIA registration placards. One, they said, had been cited twice in 2024 for operating without a permit.

"During Homecoming? Nothing," Marie Hennessey said. "It's like they just decided the rules don't apply when it's busy."

Escambia County Sheriff's Office records show 47 noise complaints and 12 parking violations issued on Pensacola Beach during the July 11-13, 2025 show weekend. That's consistent with prior years. What changed, according to multiple residents who spoke with The Flightline, was the absence of any SRIA enforcement presence.

The Money Question

Licensed short-term rental operators on Pensacola Beach pay annual registration fees ranging from $300 to $1,200 depending on property size. They submit to fire safety inspections. They carry minimum insurance coverage. They collect and remit tourist development taxes.

Unlicensed operators skip those costs. During peak demand periods like Homecoming, the rental premium can reach $800 to $1,500 per night for beachfront properties, according to listings reviewed on Airbnb and VRBO during the 2025 show.

Janet McPherson operates two licensed rentals on Via de Luna. She served on the SRIA's short-term rental advisory committee in 2023.

"We followed the rules," McPherson said. "We paid our fees. We did our inspections. And then we have to compete with people who didn't do any of that, and the county just looks the other way because it's a big weekend?"

McPherson said she raised the enforcement question at a public SRIA meeting in September 2025. The minutes from that meeting, which The Flightline obtained, show her comment was noted but no board member responded directly.

A Pattern or an Exception?

The 2025 Homecoming pause may not be unique. Three residents told The Flightline they observed similar enforcement gaps during the 2023 and 2024 shows, though none filed formal complaints at the time.

SRIA enforcement records obtained through a separate public records request show the agency issued 23 short-term rental citations in June 2025 and 31 in August 2025. For July 2025, the month containing Homecoming weekend, the agency issued four citations total. All four were dated after July 20.

The gap is not conclusive. Enforcement activity fluctuates based on complaints, staffing, and inspector availability. But the timing has drawn attention from beach residents who feel their concerns go unheard.

"If there's a policy, tell us," said Carswell. "If they decided to pause enforcement for the show, fine, but say so. Don't pretend the rules still apply when they don't."

What Comes Next

The 2026 Blue Angels Homecoming Air Show is scheduled for July 10-12. Booking data from vacation rental platforms shows beachfront properties already commanding $600 to $1,200 per night for that weekend.

The SRIA board meets monthly. Its next public meeting is April 23 at the SRIA office on Via de Luna. Short-term rental enforcement is not currently on the published agenda.

Residents who wish to address the board may sign up for public comment at the meeting. SRIA policy limits individual speakers to three minutes.

Carswell said she plans to attend.

"Someone made a decision," she said. "I want to know who."
